title: The Macro Minute: Why would POTUS attempt to fire Fed Chair Powell?

Happy Wednesday out there team 42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dale to
present our macro minute for Wednesday,
April 15th, 2026. Hope everyone's having
a great week. So, as always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
why would POTUS attempt to fire Fed
Chair Powell? Our answer is to preserve
optionality by setting Powell up as the
fall guy to the extent the economy turns
south ahead of the midterms. He likely
knows such an unprecedented act will
encounter resistance from the judicial
branch and from an FOMC that digs in and
resists his demands for muscular policy
rates.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis, uh starting
with the growth cycle. Uh in the
Eurozone, the February industrial
production data supported our outlook
for moderating growth. In Japan, the
February core machine orders data
supported our outlook for firming
inflation. Recall that Japan's machine
tool orders are among the very best
leading indicators of the global
manufacturing cycle and signal a robust
recovery in global growth in the
quarters ahead. Nothing actionable with
respect to our Bayesian priors with the
inflation cycle. In terms of the
monetary policy cycle, President Trump
stated that he would terminate Jerome
Powell as Fed Chair if he does not exit
in time, uh while affirming that the
Department of Justice investigation into
Powell's alleged wrongdoing will proceed
without interruption.
POTUS said in an interview with Fox
Business, uh I'll have to fire him,
okay, if he's not leaving on time. I've
held back from firing him. I wanted to
fire him, but I hate to be
controversial. He further added, uh
whether it's incompetence, corruption,
or both, I think you have to find out. I
really do.
These strong remarks arrived shortly
after US prosecutors attempted an
unannounced inspection of Fed offices
Washington on Tuesday, but were refused
entry. This is consistent with our
long-held view that Fed independence
will be eroded to counter the
geopolitically driven supply demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market,
which has been our core research view
since we outlined the thesis in the
summer of 2023, uh which essentially
led us to forecast that all of Wall
Street and every institution in the
world will start to rotate out of the
bond market into gold. I believe we
nailed that. Uh the Fed is the only
institution in the world uh with a
balance sheet large and flexible enough
to fill this growing void, and an
independent and its independence must be
eroded to fill it, akin to the prior
four terms. This does, however,
complicate the medium-term policy
outlook uh by lowering the probability
of incremental monetary accommodation.
Uh nothing actionable with respect to
the fiscal policy and liquidity cycles.
And then finally, the positioning cycle.
The US administration's effort to
blockade the Strait of Hormuz risks
destabilizing the US trying to trade war
detente just weeks ahead of a planned
summit in Beijing, where President Trump
was scheduled to meet with President Xi
Jinping. Xi ended a nearly 7-week
communication hiatus on Tuesday by
cautioning that the global order is
crumbling into disarray, while
committing China to play a constructive
role in the Middle East. Meanwhile,
Beijing's foreign ministry condemned
President Trump's naval blockade as
dangerous and irresponsible. The
rhetorical escalation from Beijing is
stress testing an already tenuous
US-China equilibrium with US enforcement
actions against blockade violators
raising the probability of direct
maritime friction involving
Chinese-linked vessels and US Navy
forces, thereby complicating Beijing's
preference to avoid overt confrontation.
Recall that China sources roughly 11% of
its crude oil imports from Iran.
Transition to our 42 macro dashboard, as
always, a wrap up with a question from
our community. This one's titled jobless
thesis and corporate profits. This is
for corporate profits to increase,
corporations need to buy. And
white-collar jobs get displaced by AI
agents and incomes continue to be
marginalized. When and where do we cross
the Rubicon where corporate profits can
no longer go up because there's not
enough dollars within the consumer base
to support um an increase in corporate
profits. What should we expect to see in
the models prior to it happening, i.e.
corporate profits uh falling? Excellent
question to me. I think this is the
biggest question of our time is is is is
is you know, how the economy evolves
from a profitability standpoint, what
happens to uh labor income, and
ultimately what happens to federal uh
budget uh balances uh and then to the
extent that labor income is replaced by
something like universal uh basic
income. Um that's a big question. I
don't think we have time to answer that
today, uh but the one thing I will say
as it relates to this question
specifically, when do we cross the
Rubicon? In my opinion, uh with this
chart, let me briefly summarize this
chart. The blue line of this chart shows
labor share of national income. There's
been a lot of assault on labor share of
income for decades for a variety of very
um corporatist uh policies, uh not the
least of which is globalization. Uh
obviously, we saw uh the advent of China
joining the WTO, uh things of that
nature, a lot of tax law changes
favoring capital over labor, uh a lot of
regulation that um you know, kind of
forced this line down as well. Uh then
the red line shows corporate profits as
a share of GDP, obviously up up and to
the uh right. Um so, the one thing I
will say is, you know, as long as
corporations
and and you think about this from a
revenue perspective, this question
really comes from the perspective of the
revenue angle, you know, when will
there's not be enough income to support
revenue growth uh in the in in broader
GDP growth in the economy, gross
domestic income growth. And my response
to that is, it actually does this is not
a revenue problem. This is more of a
margin story. If the blue line goes down
faster, which is labor share of national
income, if the blue line goes down
faster than the hypothetical third line,
let's say we have a yellow line here,
which is national income, if they go if
the slope of the blue line is more
negative than the slope of the uh gross
domestic income, then you're still
talking about expanding margins and and
widening profits. And so, you know,
corporations can win uh twofold. One, AI
just booms the economy, we don't have a
jobless recovery, and ultimately uh you
know, the incomes continue to to to to
to to grow and/or stagnate, at least
stagnate, but continue to grow. And then
you uh wind up having more uh dollars
flow through the bottom line from a
faster revenue growth rate. Or
conversely, you have stagnant revenue
growth, but margins expand because labor
share of that income of the income they
are receiving is actually falling faster
than the uh growth rate of that income.
Uh in the world draconian scenario, both
labor share and the income growth are
negative. And so, that's um those are
two ways in which corporations are
likely to win. And the third thing I'll
say about this is is that
AI, the the sort of foisting of AI upon
society at the quickest pace possible
and fabricating this, you know, this war
this race with China, like we must win
AI this AI race against China, which is
justifying the the the foisting the
speed at which this this technology is
being foisted upon our uh society
without any thoughtful guardrails. But I
think in my opinion, the reason they are
foisting it so quickly is not just from
a profitability perspective in terms of
the oligarchs who control the
technology, but it's also a power
consolidation perspective. If we wind up
in [clears throat] a world where there's
AGI and you have uh you know, sort of a
large portion of the workforce on
universal basic income, just think about
the amount of power that gives to the
folks in Silicon Valley or the folks
around the the world and country that
are developing uh this AI or getting
paid to legislate uh um getting paid by
those developers uh to to legislate
policies that are favorable uh for that.
I mean, to me, it's more it's more than
just profits. It's also power uh and the
dominance of power on an enduring basis.
And so, I think you have to think about
that as well in terms of why this
technology is being foisted upon us so
quickly. So, we'll wrap it up there.
Darius Dale here presenting our macro
minute for Wednesday, April 15th, 2026.
Uh just wanted to quickly shout out uh
our one of my uh mentors from from the
great beyond, uh Jackie Robinson. Happy
Jackie Robinson uh Day. Um you know, a
little bit over 5 years ago, I I I got
you know, struck with with with the
quote, you know, the life is not
important except for the impact that it
has on other lives. And it really sort
of set me down a different path from a
life perspective uh and from a from a
career perspective as well. Um you know,
the number one thing I realized at that
time when I when I came upon that quote
is that, you know, I was basically just
spending my career, you know, making
myself rich through the through helping
other rich people get rich. And that was
fine, it was great. I made a lot of
money, but the
reality is is that's not a meaningful
life to live.
And once I recognized how meaningless of
a life that was to live, uh that's when
I uh decided to um you know, create this
this wonderful company 42 macro and
serving of the thousands of customers
that we serve on a daily basis around
the world, the families that we serve.
And I'm so really super proud of what we
built here. Super proud of you guys.
Just really thankful for you guys tuning
in these macro minutes and and
supporting me in this mission to sort of
change the world and and upgrade the
investing habits of the general
investing public. Um and so, just thank
you for all that. And a happy Jackie
Robinson Day. We'll catch you back here
tomorrow. Cheers.
